topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the coffee shop suddenly felt thick, suffocating. Sarah watched Mark lean back in his chair, a careless, easy smile plastered across his face as he described his 'innovative' start-up venture. Every word he spoke about his role as a senior software developer felt like a calculated performance. When I brought up the project deadline, he stammered, a flicker of panic darting across his eyes before he smoothly pivoted to a vague explanation about 'consulting.' My jaw tightened. I could feel a hot flush creeping up my neck, and I gripped my mug so tightly my knuckles turned white. The casual camaraderie we usually shared had evaporated, replaced by a cold, sharp sense of being completely out of the loop. I forced a small, tight laugh, a sound that felt brittle and entirely untrue, and quickly looked down at my hands, focusing intensely on the condensation dripping down the ceramic.

[story 2]

I walked into the office lobby, my heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. Liam was waiting by the water cooler, looking annoyingly relaxed. He started recounting his day, detailing complex client meetings and quarterly reports with such practiced confidence. Then, I brought up the specific project we had discussed weeks ago—the one he claimed was his main focus. His eyes flickered away, darting towards the reception desk before snapping back with an overly bright expression. The silence stretched, heavy and accusatory. I felt a strange pressure building behind my sternum, a tightening sensation that made breathing shallow. When he finally admitted, in a rush of nervous chatter, that he was actually just an intern shadowing someone else, the color seemed to drain from my face. I mumbled something about needing to grab a quick lunch, the sound barely audible, and walked away, needing the physical distance to process the sudden, jarring disconnect between what I believed and what was actually happening.

[story 3]

It happened during a casual lunch. Maya was animated, gesturing wildly about her promotion, describing the prestige of her new position at the design firm. I nodded along, trying to appear interested, but my attention kept snagging on the slight hesitation in her voice whenever she mentioned specific responsibilities. When I posed a question about the proprietary software she was using, she faltered, her gaze dropping to her plate. The atmosphere around her shifted; the light seemed to dim around her as she constructed a narrative that didn't quite align with the reality I was observing. When she finally admitted, with a sigh that seemed too dramatic, that she was essentially just an administrative assistant with a title upgrade, a profound sense of awkwardness settled over me. I found myself staring at the pattern on the tablecloth, my shoulders slumping forward as if weighted down by an invisible burden. Every muscle in my body felt tense, a knot of profound awkwardness refusing to loosen.
